enrichment.short_description: Teamwork.com is a client-work and project management platform built for agencies, consultancies, and professional-services teams that need project planning, time tracking, and client billing in one connected system.
enrichment.seo_title: Teamwork.com Review: Features, Pricing and Plans
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what Teamwork.com offers agencies and client-services teams: project views, time tracking, client billing, pricing plans, and integrations.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Teamwork (Teamwork.com) is a project and client-work management platform founded in 2007 by Peter Coppinger and Daniel Mackey in Cork, Ireland, where the company remains headquartered. It is purpose-built for agencies, consultancies, and other professional-services teams that bill clients for time and need to manage multiple projects at once. The platform combines task management, Gantt-style timeline views, time tracking, resource and capacity planning, budgeting, invoicing, and client-facing proofing tools, plus an AI assistant called Teamwork AI. It integrates with tools such as HubSpot, QuickBooks, Xero, Slack, and Google Workspace. Pricing runs on a freemium model: a Free plan supports up to 5 users and 5 projects, while paid tiers include Basics (from about $9.99 per user per month billed yearly), Accelerate (from about $24.99 per user per month billed yearly) with budgeting and advanced automation, and custom-priced Optimize and Enterprise tiers with CRM integrations, resource scheduling by role, and SSO. Teamwork.com serves more than 20,000 teams across roughly 170 countries and has raised outside investment, including $70 million from Bregal Milestone in 2021, while remaining largely founder-led.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Teamwork.com
paragraphs: Teamwork.com is a project management and client-work platform designed specifically for agencies, consultancies, and other teams that deliver billable work for external clients. Unlike general-purpose task tools, it bundles project planning with time tracking, budgeting, and invoicing so that client-facing teams can manage delivery and profitability from a single system.

Founded in 2007 by Peter Coppinger and Daniel Mackey in Cork, Ireland, the company grew as a bootstrapped business before taking a $70 million growth investment from Bregal Milestone in 2021. Teamwork.com now serves tens of thousands of teams worldwide, with particular strength among marketing agencies, software consultancies, and creative studios that need to track time against client budgets.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform offers multiple project views, including list, board (kanban), table, calendar, and Gantt-style timeline views, so teams can plan and track work in the format that suits them.

Built-in time tracking lets users log billable and non-billable hours, generate timesheets, and sync entries directly into client invoices, which is one of the features that differentiates Teamwork.com from generic project management tools.
Resource and capacity planning tools help managers see who is over or under allocated across active projects, while budgeting features track project profitability in real time.

Teamwork AI adds AI-assisted features such as automated status updates, summarization, and workflow suggestions across paid plans, alongside an automation engine with a monthly cap on automated actions that scales by plan.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Teamwork.com uses a freemium pricing model with five tiers. The Free plan supports up to 5 users and 5 projects with core views and a limited automation allowance. The Basics plan starts at roughly $9.99 per user per month billed yearly and adds unlimited templates, time tracking, and additional views.

The Accelerate plan starts at roughly $24.99 per user per month billed yearly and unlocks smart forms, higher automation limits, capacity planning, time budgets, and integrations with HubSpot and QuickBooks. Optimize and Enterprise are custom-priced tiers aimed at larger organizations, adding CRM pipeline integration, multi-currency budgeting, Salesforce and NetSuite integration, single sign-on, and a dedicated customer success manager. Annual billing saves roughly 20 to 29 percent compared with monthly billing across paid tiers.

features.description: Switch between list, board, table, calendar, and Gantt-style timeline views for the same project data.

features.title: Built-in time tracking

features.description: Generate and send invoices from tracked time and project budgets, with syncing to Xero and QuickBooks.

features.title: Resource and capacity planning
features.description: Visualize team workload and availability to avoid over-booking staff across concurrent client projects.

features.title: Budgeting and profitability tracking
features.description: Track project budgets against actual hours and costs to see profitability in real time.

features.title: Teamwork AI
features.description: AI-assisted summaries, status updates, and workflow suggestions built into paid plans.

features.title: Automation engine
features.description: Rule-based automations that trigger status changes, notifications, and task assignments, with monthly action limits by plan.

features.title: Client and proofing tools
features.description: Client users can be added for visibility, feedback, and file proofing without full team-member access.

pros: Combines project management with native time tracking and invoicing, reducing the need for separate billing tools
Strong fit for agencies and client-services teams managing many concurrent projects and budgets
Multiple project views (Gantt, board, list, table) suit different planning styles on the same data
Resource and capacity planning tools help prevent team over-allocation
Generous free tier for very small teams to evaluate the platform
cons: Per-user pricing can get expensive for larger teams once advanced features are needed
Some advanced features such as CRM integration and multi-currency budgeting are locked behind custom-priced Optimize and Enterprise tiers
Automation actions are capped by plan, which can be limiting for heavy automation users on lower tiers
Interface has a learning curve for teams new to full-featured project management software
Mobile app is generally reported as less capable than the desktop web experience

faqs.question: What is Teamwork.com used for
faqs.answer: Teamwork.com is used for planning and tracking client-facing projects, including task management, time tracking, resource planning, budgeting, and invoicing, primarily by agencies and professional-services teams.

faqs.question: Does Teamwork.com have a free plan
faqs.answer: Yes, Teamwork.com offers a Free plan for up to 5 users and 5 projects with core project views and a limited automation allowance.

faqs.question: How much does Teamwork.com cost
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at roughly $9.99 per user per month billed yearly for the Basics plan, with the Accelerate plan starting at roughly $24.99 per user per month billed yearly, and custom pricing for Optimize and Enterprise tiers.

faqs.question: Who founded Teamwork.com
faqs.answer: Teamwork.com was founded in 2007 by Peter Coppinger and Daniel Mackey in Cork, Ireland, where the company is still headquartered.

faqs.question: Does Teamwork.com support time tracking and invoicing
faqs.answer: Yes, native time tracking, timesheets, and invoicing that syncs with Xero and QuickBooks are built into the platform.

faqs.question: Is Teamwork.com good for agencies
faqs.answer: Teamwork.com is widely used by marketing, creative, and software agencies because it combines project delivery tracking with client billing and profitability reporting in one system.

faqs.question: Does Teamwork.com integrate with other tools
faqs.answer: Yes, it integrates with tools such as HubSpot, QuickBooks, Xero, Slack, Google Workspace, and, on higher tiers, Salesforce and NetSuite.

faqs.question: What is Teamwork AI
faqs.answer: Teamwork AI is a set of AI-assisted features built into paid plans that help summarize project activity, draft status updates, and suggest workflow actions.

enrichment.short_description: Tebra is a healthcare practice management and EHR platform formed from the 2021 merger of Kareo and PatientPop, combining clinical, billing, and patient-growth tools for independent medical practices.
enrichment.starting_price: Custom quote, commonly reported from about $49/provider/month
enrichment.headquarters: Corona del Mar, California, United States
enrichment.seo_title: Tebra Review: Practice Management and EHR Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Tebra combines EHR, billing, scheduling, and patient-growth tools for independent practices. See features, pricing, pros, and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Tebra is a digital healthcare technology company formed in November 2021 through the merger of Kareo, an EHR and medical billing company founded by Dan Rodrigues in 2004, and PatientPop, a practice growth and patient engagement platform based in Santa Monica, California. Tebra is headquartered in Corona del Mar, California, and is led by Kareo founder Dan Rodrigues as CEO. The platform serves independent healthcare practices, integrating electronic health records (EHR), practice management, medical billing and revenue cycle management, telehealth, online scheduling, patient engagement messaging, and practice marketing tools such as websites and reputation management, all in one connected system. As of the mid-2020s, Tebra reports serving over 140,000 healthcare providers covering more than 120 million patients in the United States, with roughly 1,000 to 1,100 employees. Tebra does not publish fixed public pricing; the company uses a custom-quote model based on the number of providers and modules selected, with reported real-world quotes ranging from roughly $49 to $799 per provider per month depending on whether a practice buys a single module or a full platform bundle such as Practice Essentials or Practice Automation. Additional costs include card payment processing fees and optional AI note-generation add-ons.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Tebra
paragraphs: Tebra is a healthcare technology platform for independent medical practices, formed in November 2021 when Kareo, an established EHR and medical billing company, merged with PatientPop, a practice-growth and patient-engagement platform. The combined company is headquartered in Corona del Mar, California, and is led by Kareo founder Dan Rodrigues as CEO.

The platform is designed to give independent practices the same operational and marketing capabilities as larger health systems, spanning clinical documentation, billing, scheduling, telehealth, and patient acquisition, without requiring practices to stitch together multiple point solutions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Tebra's EHR module supports clinical documentation, e-prescribing, and certified electronic health records for a range of specialties, alongside AI-assisted note generation as an add-on.

Billing and revenue cycle management tools handle insurance claims, patient payments, and denial management, with card payment processing available directly through Tebra Payments or via Stripe integration.
Patient engagement features include online scheduling, automated appointment reminders, digital patient registration, and two-way messaging.

Practice marketing tools carried over from PatientPop include practice websites, search marketing, and online reputation and review management, aimed at helping independent practices attract new patients.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Tebra does not publish fixed public pricing tiers. Instead, pricing is quote-based and built around the number of clinical providers rather than total staff seats, meaning support staff can typically use the platform at no additional per-seat cost.

Practices can start with a single solution (such as billing or EHR alone) or choose a platform bundle: Practice Essentials, which combines billing, clinical EHR, and telehealth, or Practice Automation, which adds patient experience and engagement tools on top. Reported real-world quotes range from roughly $49 to $799 per provider per month depending on modules and practice size, with full-bundle quotes commonly falling between about $149 and $499 per provider per month. Card processing (2.75% plus $0.30 through Tebra Payments, or 2.9% plus $0.30 via Stripe) and optional AI note generation (around $99 per provider per month, or usage-based per note) are billed separately.

features.title: Certified EHR
features.description: Clinical documentation, e-prescribing, and certified electronic health records supporting multiple specialties.

features.title: Medical billing and revenue cycle management
features.description: Insurance claims submission, patient payments, and denial management tools built into the platform.

features.title: Online scheduling and reminders
features.description: Patient-facing scheduling with automated appointment reminders to reduce no-shows.

features.title: Telehealth
features.description: Integrated video visits as part of the Practice Essentials bundle.

features.title: Practice websites and marketing
features.description: Website building, search marketing, and online reputation management inherited from PatientPop.

features.title: Patient messaging and digital registration
features.description: Two-way patient messaging and digital intake forms to reduce front-desk paperwork.

features.title: AI note generation
features.description: Optional AI-assisted clinical note generation available as a capped subscription or per-note add-on.

features.title: Provider-based pricing
features.description: Pricing is based on the number of clinical providers rather than total staff, so support staff can typically use the platform at no extra seat cost.

pros: Combines EHR, billing, scheduling, and marketing in one platform, reducing the need for multiple vendors
Provider-based pricing means non-clinical staff can generally use the system without extra per-seat charges
Strong practice-marketing toolset inherited from PatientPop, including websites and reputation management
Serves a large base of independent practices, giving the platform mature, specialty-tested workflows
Modular bundles let practices start with core needs and add modules over time
cons: No published fixed pricing, so practices must go through a sales quote process to know exact costs
Reported pricing varies widely (roughly $49 to $799 per provider per month) depending on modules and negotiation
Payment processing and AI note generation are billed as additional costs on top of the base subscription
Some users report a learning curve when migrating from legacy Kareo or PatientPop-only setups to the unified platform
As with any merged platform, some legacy Kareo and PatientPop features have required time to fully integrate

faqs.question: What is Tebra
faqs.answer: Tebra is a healthcare practice management and EHR platform formed in 2021 from the merger of Kareo and PatientPop, serving independent medical practices with clinical, billing, and patient-growth tools.

faqs.question: How much does Tebra cost
faqs.answer: Tebra uses custom quote-based pricing tied to the number of providers, with reported costs commonly ranging from about $49 to $799 per provider per month depending on the modules selected.

faqs.question: Is Tebra the same as Kareo
faqs.answer: Kareo is one of the two companies, along with PatientPop, that merged in November 2021 to form Tebra. Kareo's EHR and billing technology now operates as part of the Tebra platform.

faqs.question: Does Tebra include an EHR
faqs.answer: Yes, Tebra includes a certified electronic health record module for clinical documentation and e-prescribing alongside its billing and practice management tools.

faqs.question: Who founded Tebra
faqs.answer: Tebra was formed by the 2021 merger of Kareo, founded by Dan Rodrigues in 2004, and PatientPop. Dan Rodrigues serves as CEO of Tebra.

faqs.question: Where is Tebra headquartered
faqs.answer: Tebra is headquartered in Corona del Mar, California, United States.

faqs.question: Does Tebra charge per provider or per user
faqs.answer: Tebra's pricing model is based on the number of clinical providers rather than total staff seats.

faqs.question: Does Tebra offer telehealth
faqs.answer: Yes, telehealth is included as part of Tebra's Practice Essentials platform bundle.

enrichment.short_description: Technitium DNS Server is a free, open-source, self-hosted authoritative and recursive DNS server for privacy, ad-blocking, and network-level DNS control, built with a browser-based admin console.
enrichment.seo_title: Technitium DNS Server: Free Self-Hosted DNS Software
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Technitium DNS Server is a free, open-source, self-hosted DNS server with DNS-over-HTTPS, ad blocking, and a web admin console.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Technitium DNS Server is a free and open-source, cross-platform authoritative and recursive DNS server created by Shreyas Zare, a developer based in Mumbai, India, who also built the Technitium MAC Address Changer. The DNS server is distributed under the GNU GPLv3 license, meaning the full feature set, including recursive resolution, authoritative zone hosting, DNS-over-TLS, DNS-over-HTTPS (with HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3 support), and DNS-over-QUIC, is available at no cost with no paid or feature-limited edition. It ships with a browser-based web admin console, a built-in DHCP server for multiple networks, clustering support to manage several DNS server instances from one console, multi-user role-based access control, and TOTP-based two-factor authentication. Built on .NET, it is designed to run efficiently on modest hardware, from a Raspberry Pi to a commodity desktop PC, while handling high query volumes using asynchronous I/O. Common use cases include self-hosted network-wide ad and tracker blocking, private DNS resolution for home labs and small businesses, and privacy-focused alternatives to public DNS resolvers. As open-source software with no company behind it in the venture-funded sense, Technitium has no disclosed company size, funding, or enterprise pricing tiers; it is maintained as an independent open-source project with active GitHub development and a community blog documenting frequent releases.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Technitium DNS Server
paragraphs: Technitium DNS Server is a free, open-source DNS server that individuals and organizations can self-host for privacy, security, and network-level control over DNS resolution. It functions as both an authoritative DNS server for hosting your own zones and a recursive resolver for answering queries on behalf of client devices.

The project was created by Shreyas Zare, an independent developer based in Mumbai, India, who has maintained Technitium's suite of networking tools, including the DNS server and the earlier Technitium MAC Address Changer, since the mid-2000s. The DNS server itself has been actively developed with frequent releases documented on the Technitium blog.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The server works out of the box with minimal configuration and includes a browser-based web admin console for managing zones, forwarders, and blocklists without editing configuration files by hand.

It supports encrypted DNS protocols including DNS-over-TLS, DNS-over-HTTPS (HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3), and DNS-over-QUIC, letting administrators run their own encrypted DNS endpoint rather than relying on third-party resolvers.
A built-in DHCP server can manage multiple networks, and a clustering feature allows several DNS server instances to be administered from a single console for redundancy and load distribution.
Security features include multi-user, role-based access control, non-expiring API tokens for automation, and TOTP-based two-factor authentication for admin accounts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Technitium DNS Server is entirely free and released under the GNU GPLv3 open-source license, which permits both personal and commercial use. There is no paid tier, license key, or feature gate; every capability, including clustering, DHCP, and encrypted DNS protocols, is available in the free download.

Because it is self-hosted, the only ongoing cost is the infrastructure used to run it, such as a home server, Raspberry Pi, virtual machine, or cloud instance. Users who redistribute modified versions of the software are required under GPLv3 to make their source code changes available under the same license.

features.title: Recursive and authoritative DNS
features.description: Runs as both a recursive resolver and an authoritative server for hosting your own DNS zones.

features.title: Encrypted DNS protocols
features.description: Supports DNS-over-TLS, DNS-over-HTTPS, and DNS-over-QUIC for private, encrypted DNS resolution.

features.title: Web-based admin console
features.description: Browser-based interface for configuration, zone management, and monitoring, including a dark mode option.

features.title: Built-in DHCP server
features.description: Manages DHCP for multiple networks alongside DNS from the same application.

features.title: Clustering
features.description: Manage two or more DNS Server instances from a single admin console for redundancy.

features.title: Ad and tracker blocking
features.description: Built-in support for blocklists to filter ads, trackers, and malicious domains network-wide.

features.title: Role-based access and 2FA
features.description: Multi-user accounts with role-based permissions and TOTP two-factor authentication.

features.title: High-performance async architecture
features.description: Built on .NET with asynchronous I/O to handle high query volumes on commodity hardware.

pros: Completely free and open source with no feature-limited paid tier
Full encrypted DNS protocol support (DoT, DoH, DoQ) built in, not requiring third-party add-ons
Web-based admin console makes it more approachable than many self-hosted DNS tools
Combines DNS and DHCP server functionality in a single application
Actively maintained with frequent releases and an engaged self-hosting community
cons: Requires self-hosting and basic networking knowledge to deploy and maintain
No official enterprise support contracts or SLAs since it is a community-maintained open-source project
Documentation is community-driven and less polished than some commercial DNS platforms
Being a single-developer-led project, long-term roadmap depends on one maintainer's continued involvement
Advanced features like clustering require more setup effort than plug-and-play SaaS DNS filtering tools

faqs.question: Is Technitium DNS Server free
faqs.answer: Yes, Technitium DNS Server is completely free and open source under the GNU GPLv3 license, with no paid tier or feature limits.

faqs.question: Who created Technitium DNS Server
faqs.answer: Technitium DNS Server was created by Shreyas Zare, an independent developer based in Mumbai, India.

faqs.question: Can Technitium DNS Server block ads network-wide
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports blocklists that filter ads, trackers, and malicious domains for every device on the network using it.

faqs.question: Does Technitium DNS Server support DNS-over-HTTPS
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports DNS-over-HTTPS along with DNS-over-TLS and DNS-over-QUIC for encrypted DNS resolution.

faqs.question: What platforms does Technitium DNS Server run on
faqs.answer: It is a cross-platform application built on .NET and can run on Windows, Linux, macOS, and low-power devices such as a Raspberry Pi.

faqs.question: Does Technitium DNS Server include a DHCP server
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes a built-in DHCP server capable of managing multiple networks.

faqs.question: Is Technitium DNS Server suitable for businesses
faqs.answer: Yes, its GPLv3 license permits commercial use, and features like clustering and role-based access support small business and self-hosted enterprise use, though there is no official commercial support contract.

faqs.question: How is Technitium DNS Server different from public resolvers like Cloudflare or Google DNS
faqs.answer: Unlike third-party public resolvers, Technitium DNS Server is self-hosted, meaning the administrator controls the resolver, its logs, and its blocking rules directly rather than relying on an external provider.

use_cases.title: Home network DNS and ad blocking
use_cases.description: Self-hosters run Technitium on a home server or Raspberry Pi to block ads and trackers network-wide and gain visibility into DNS queries.
use_cases.audience: Home lab enthusiasts and privacy-focused individuals

use_cases.title: Small business private DNS resolution
use_cases.description: Small organizations self-host Technitium as an internal DNS resolver with encrypted DNS and DHCP management.

use_cases.title: Authoritative DNS hosting for personal domains
use_cases.description: Users host their own DNS zones for personal or project domains without relying on a third-party DNS provider.

enrichment.short_description: Teedy is a lightweight, open-source document management system (formerly Sismics Docs) offering full-text search, OCR, encryption, and workflow tools, available as a free self-hosted app or a paid cloud service.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted); cloud plans from about EUR 19/user/month
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Teedy is a free, open-source document management system with OCR, encryption, and search, plus an optional paid cloud version.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Teedy is a lightweight, open-source document management system built by Sismics, a software company based in Lyon, France, and originally released as Sismics Docs before being rebranded to Teedy in 2016. It is built on Java with a modular Maven architecture and offers a responsive web interface, a REST API, and an Android client. Core features include OCR-based text extraction, 256-bit AES encryption, full-text search, granular user and group permissions, tagging, and basic workflow support. The self-hosted, open-source version is released under the GPL license and is completely free, including all features such as OCR and encryption, with the only cost being the infrastructure used to run it; a preconfigured Docker image is available, and it can run without external dependencies using an embedded H2 database, or with PostgreSQL for larger deployments. The project has been reported to scale to roughly one million documents in production use, and can be deployed on hardware ranging from a Raspberry Pi to Kubernetes clusters. Sismics also offers a commercial, hosted cloud version at teedy.io for teams that do not want to self-host, with pricing starting at roughly EUR 19 per user per month. Teedy's interface supports English, French, Simplified Chinese, and Traditional Chinese.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Teedy
paragraphs: Teedy is a lightweight, open-source document management system created by Sismics, a software company based in Lyon, France. It began life as Sismics Docs and was rebranded to Teedy in 2016, though the underlying open-source project remains hosted on GitHub under the sismics/docs repository.

The system is designed to bring enterprise-grade document management features, such as OCR, encryption, and full-text search, to individuals and small businesses without the cost of large proprietary document management platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Teedy automatically extracts text from scanned documents and images using OCR, making uploaded files searchable even when they originate as scans or photos.
Documents can be encrypted with 256-bit AES encryption, and access is controlled through granular user and group permission settings.
A full-text search engine indexes document content and metadata, letting users quickly locate files across large document sets.
The platform includes tagging, basic workflow and routing support, a REST API for integrations, and a native Android client for mobile access.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The self-hosted, open-source version of Teedy is completely free under the GPL license, including every feature such as OCR, encryption, and workflow tools. Users only pay for the infrastructure needed to host it, whether that is a home server, a Raspberry Pi, or a cloud virtual machine, and a preconfigured Docker image is available to simplify deployment.

For teams that prefer not to manage their own infrastructure, Sismics also offers a commercial, hosted cloud version of Teedy at teedy.io, with published pricing starting at roughly EUR 19 per user per month.

features.title: OCR text extraction
features.description: Automatically extracts searchable text from scanned documents and images.

features.title: 256-bit AES encryption
features.description: Encrypts stored documents to protect sensitive files.

features.title: Full-text search
features.description: Indexes document content and metadata for fast retrieval across large archives.

features.title: Granular permissions
features.description: User and group-based access control for documents and folders.

features.title: REST API
features.description: Programmatic access for integrating Teedy with other systems and custom workflows.

features.title: Android client
features.description: Native mobile app for accessing and uploading documents on the go.

features.title: Docker-first deployment
features.description: Preconfigured Docker image with OCR and media conversion tools included, ready to run on port 8080.

features.title: Tagging and workflow tools
features.description: Organize documents with tags and route them through basic approval or review workflows.

pros: Free and open source under GPL with no feature limitations on the self-hosted version
Includes enterprise-style features like OCR and encryption typically found only in paid document management systems
Lightweight enough to run on modest hardware such as a Raspberry Pi, while also scaling to Kubernetes
Docker-first deployment makes self-hosting straightforward
Optional hosted cloud version available for teams that do not want to manage infrastructure
cons: Self-hosting requires basic server administration skills to deploy, secure, and maintain
Smaller ecosystem and community compared to larger commercial document management platforms
Default embedded H2 database is best suited for smaller deployments; PostgreSQL is recommended for scale
Mobile experience is limited to an Android client, with no native iOS app confirmed
Cloud version pricing and feature parity with self-hosted may vary and require checking current plans

faqs.question: Is Teedy free
faqs.answer: Yes, the self-hosted, open-source version of Teedy is free under the GPL license. Sismics also offers an optional paid cloud version starting at roughly EUR 19 per user per month.

faqs.question: What was Teedy called before
faqs.answer: Teedy was originally released as Sismics Docs before being rebranded to Teedy in 2016.

faqs.question: Does Teedy support OCR
faqs.answer: Yes, Teedy extracts text from scanned documents and images using OCR so they become searchable.

faqs.question: Can Teedy be self-hosted
faqs.answer: Yes, Teedy is designed to be self-hosted using a Docker image, and can run on hardware ranging from a Raspberry Pi to Kubernetes clusters.

faqs.question: What database does Teedy use
faqs.answer: Teedy uses an embedded H2 database by default if no external database is configured, and supports PostgreSQL for larger deployments.

faqs.question: Does Teedy have a mobile app
faqs.answer: Teedy offers a native Android client for mobile access to documents.

faqs.question: Who develops Teedy
faqs.answer: Teedy is developed by Sismics, a software company based in Lyon, France.

faqs.question: What languages does Teedy support
faqs.answer: Teedy's interface supports English, French, Simplified Chinese, and Traditional Chinese.

use_cases.title: Small business document archiving
use_cases.description: Small teams self-host Teedy to digitize, OCR, and organize business documents like invoices and contracts.

use_cases.title: Personal document management
use_cases.description: Individuals self-host Teedy on a home server or Raspberry Pi to organize personal records with search and encryption.

use_cases.title: Team knowledge and file repository
use_cases.description: Teams use Teedy's permissions, tagging, and workflow features to manage shared documents and approvals.

enrichment.short_description: Teleport (goteleport.com) is an infrastructure identity and access security platform that replaces passwords, SSH keys, and static credentials with short-lived, cryptographically verified access to servers, Kubernetes, databases, and cloud consoles.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Community Edition); paid plans from about $10/user/month
enrichment.seo_title: Teleport: Infrastructure Access and Identity Security
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Teleport secures access to servers, Kubernetes, databases, and cloud infrastructure with identity-based, credential-free access. See pricing and features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Teleport, built by the company originally named Gravitational and now doing business as Teleport, Inc., is an infrastructure identity and access security platform founded in 2015 by CEO Ev Kontsevoy, CTO Alexander (Sasha) Klizhentas, and COO Taylor Wakefield. Headquartered in Oakland, California, the company has raised roughly $169 million in funding from investors including Kleiner Perkins, Bessemer Venture Partners, Y Combinator, and Insight Partners, reaching unicorn status with a valuation around $1.1 billion in 2022. Teleport replaces passwords, SSH keys, API tokens, and static database credentials with short-lived, cryptographically signed certificates, providing zero trust, least-privileged access to Linux servers, Windows desktops, Kubernetes clusters, databases, internal web applications, cloud provider consoles (AWS, Azure, GCP), and, more recently, Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers used by AI agents. A free, open-source Community Edition (built on a modified Apache 2.0 license, with commercial-use restrictions for larger companies since version 16) covers core SSH, Kubernetes, and database access for hobbyists and small teams. Paid tiers include a Team plan reported around $10 per user per month and a Business plan reported around $18 per user per month, plus custom-priced Enterprise and self-hosted options for large organizations. Teleport reports more than 600 customers, including Elastic, Samsung, NASDAQ, and IBM, and employs roughly 250 to 270 people.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Teleport
paragraphs: Teleport is an infrastructure identity and access security platform, sometimes categorized as privileged access management (PAM), that lets organizations grant employees, services, and now AI agents secure access to servers, cloud consoles, databases, and internal applications without relying on static, long-lived credentials.

The company was founded in 2015 by Ev Kontsevoy, Alexander (Sasha) Klizhentas, and Taylor Wakefield, and is headquartered in Oakland, California. It began as an open-source project (originally under the name Gravitational) and has grown into a venture-backed company valued at roughly $1.1 billion, used by more than 600 customers including Elastic, Samsung, NASDAQ, and IBM.

Note: Teleport (goteleport.com) is an entirely separate company from TeleportHQ (teleporthq.io), a no-code visual web app builder based in Romania. Despite the similar name, the two products, companies, and founding teams are unrelated.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Teleport issues short-lived, cryptographically signed certificates instead of passwords or long-lived SSH keys and API tokens, minimizing the risk of credential theft or sprawl.

It provides unified, zero trust access to a broad set of infrastructure, including Linux servers, Windows desktops, Kubernetes clusters, databases, internal web apps, and cloud provider APIs and consoles for AWS, Azure, and GCP.
Session recording and detailed audit logging give security teams visibility into exactly who accessed what infrastructure and when, simplifying compliance audits.

Newer capabilities extend Teleport's access model to Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers, letting organizations apply the same identity-based access controls to AI agents interacting with infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Teleport offers a free, open-source Community Edition suitable for hobbyists, homelabs, and small-scale self-hosted deployments. As of Teleport 16, Community Edition builds carry a modified Apache 2.0 commercial license that restricts free commercial use to companies with fewer than 100 employees and less than $10 million in annual revenue; individuals can use it for personal or hobby purposes without restriction.

Paid plans are usage-based, charging for active users and protected resources. Reported list pricing for a Team-level plan is around $10 per user per month, with a Business-level plan around $18 per user per month. Enterprise and self-hosted deployments for larger organizations use custom pricing, and exact current pricing requires contacting Teleport's sales team or requesting the published pricing guide.

features.title: Certificate-based access
features.description: Replaces passwords, SSH keys, and API tokens with short-lived, cryptographically signed certificates.

features.title: Unified infrastructure access
features.description: Single platform for accessing Linux servers, Windows desktops, Kubernetes clusters, databases, and internal web apps.

features.title: Cloud console access
features.description: Secure, credential-free access to AWS, Azure, and GCP consoles and APIs.

features.title: Session recording and audit logs
features.description: Records sessions and logs access activity for security review and compliance audits.

features.title: Zero trust, least-privileged access
features.description: Grants ephemeral, scoped access rather than standing privileged credentials.

features.title: Open-source Community Edition
features.description: Free self-hosted edition covering core SSH, Kubernetes, and database access for small-scale use.

features.title: MCP server access control
features.description: Extends identity-based access controls to Model Context Protocol servers used by AI agents.

features.title: Single sign-on integration
features.description: Integrates with identity providers for centralized authentication across infrastructure.

pros: Eliminates static credentials like SSH keys and passwords in favor of short-lived certificates, reducing credential-theft risk
Covers a very broad range of infrastructure types (servers, Kubernetes, databases, cloud consoles, web apps) in one platform
Free, open-source Community Edition available for small teams, hobbyists, and evaluation
Detailed session recording and audit logs simplify security compliance
Backed by well-known investors and used by large enterprises, indicating platform maturity
cons: Full public pricing is not published; getting an exact quote requires contacting sales
Community Edition's free commercial use is restricted to companies under 100 employees and under $10 million in annual revenue as of version 16
Self-hosted Enterprise deployments can involve significant annual contract minimums for larger organizations
Initial setup and infrastructure onboarding can require meaningful engineering effort for complex environments
As a security-critical system, misconfiguration carries higher operational risk than lower-stakes SaaS tools

faqs.question: What is Teleport used for
faqs.answer: Teleport is used to provide secure, credential-free access to infrastructure such as servers, Kubernetes clusters, databases, cloud consoles, and internal applications using short-lived certificates instead of passwords or static keys.

faqs.question: Is Teleport free
faqs.answer: Teleport offers a free, open-source Community Edition for small-scale and personal use. Paid Team, Business, and Enterprise plans add additional features, support, and higher usage limits.

faqs.question: Who founded Teleport
faqs.answer: Teleport was founded in 2015 by Ev Kontsevoy, Alexander (Sasha) Klizhentas, and Taylor Wakefield, and is headquartered in Oakland, California.

faqs.question: Is Teleport the same company as TeleportHQ
faqs.answer: No. Teleport (goteleport.com) is an infrastructure access and identity security company, while TeleportHQ (teleporthq.io) is an unrelated no-code visual web app builder based in Romania.

faqs.question: How much does Teleport cost
faqs.answer: Teleport's paid plans are usage-based; reported pricing starts around $10 per user per month for a Team-level plan, with Business and Enterprise tiers priced higher or custom-quoted.

faqs.question: Does Teleport support Kubernetes access
faqs.answer: Yes, Teleport provides zero trust access to Kubernetes clusters alongside servers, databases, and cloud consoles.

faqs.question: Can Teleport secure AI agent access to infrastructure
faqs.answer: Yes, Teleport has extended its access model to Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers, applying the same identity-based controls to AI agents.

faqs.question: How much funding has Teleport raised
faqs.answer: Teleport has raised roughly $169 million in funding, reaching a valuation of about $1.1 billion in 2022.

use_cases.title: Zero trust infrastructure access
use_cases.description: Engineering teams replace SSH keys and static credentials with Teleport's short-lived certificates for accessing servers and Kubernetes.

use_cases.title: Compliance-driven access auditing
use_cases.description: Security teams use Teleport's session recording and audit logs to meet compliance requirements around privileged access.
use_cases.audience: Security and compliance teams at regulated companies

use_cases.title: Secure AI agent infrastructure access
use_cases.description: Organizations apply Teleport's identity controls to MCP servers so AI agents access infrastructure under the same least-privilege model as humans.
use_cases.audience: Platform teams deploying AI agents in production

features: Core SSH, Kubernetes, and database access
Free for personal and hobby use; restricted commercial use for larger companies
Core access features for small teams
Usage-based pricing on active users and resources

enrichment.short_description: TeleportHQ is a low-code, AI-assisted visual web app builder based in Romania that lets users design interfaces visually and export production-ready code in frameworks like React, Vue, Next.js, and Angular.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (paid Professional plan from about $9/editor/month billed annually)
enrichment.seo_title: TeleportHQ: Low-Code Visual Web App Builder
enrichment.seo_meta_description: TeleportHQ is a low-code visual builder that exports production-ready React, Vue, and HTML/CSS code. See features, pricing, and plans.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: TeleportHQ is a low-code, AI-assisted visual web app and website builder founded in 2017 by CEO Paul Brie and headquartered in Cluj-Napoca, Romania. Unlike closed no-code page builders, TeleportHQ focuses on generating clean, production-ready code that developers can export in multiple frameworks, including React, Vue, Next.js, Angular, and plain HTML/CSS, alongside Figma import and AI-assisted design generation. The company raised a 2.5 million dollar seed round in 2022 led by Tokyo-based One Capital, with co-financing from the European Fund for Regional Development. TeleportHQ offers a free plan that includes 3 projects, up to 10 code views or downloads, unlimited team collaborators, free hosting on a TeleportHQ subdomain, and access to the AI website builder and Figma integration. Paid plans start with Professional, priced at roughly 9 dollars per editor per month when billed annually (18 dollars per editor per month billed monthly), which unlocks unlimited projects, unlimited code exports, private projects, and video uploads. An Agency plan adds support for 10 or more custom domains, custom storage and bandwidth, and Vercel integration, while Enterprise pricing is custom and requires contacting sales. TeleportHQ is completely unrelated to Teleport (goteleport.com), a US-based infrastructure access security company; the two share a name but not a founding team, product, or company history.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is TeleportHQ
paragraphs: TeleportHQ is a low-code, AI-assisted platform for visually designing web interfaces and exporting the resulting layouts as production-ready front-end code. It is aimed at designers, developers, and entrepreneurs who want to move quickly from design to shippable code without being locked into a proprietary hosting format.

The company was founded in 2017 by CEO Paul Brie and is headquartered in Cluj-Napoca, Romania. TeleportHQ describes its mission as making user interface creation more accessible through automation and AI, drawing on the founding team's front-end development background. In 2022, the company raised a 2.5 million dollar seed round led by Tokyo-based One Capital, with additional co-financing from the European Fund for Regional Development.

Note: TeleportHQ (teleporthq.io) is a completely separate, unrelated company from Teleport (goteleport.com), a US infrastructure access and identity security platform. The two products share only a similar name.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Users build interfaces with a visual, drag-and-drop editor and can import existing designs directly from Figma to speed up the handoff between design and code.
An AI website builder can generate initial layouts and components from prompts, which users can then refine visually before exporting.
TeleportHQ exports clean, production-ready code in multiple frameworks, including React, Vue, Next.js, Angular, and plain HTML and CSS, rather than locking output into a single proprietary format.
Projects can be published directly on a TeleportHQ subdomain for quick previews, or connected to custom domains and platforms like Vercel on higher-tier plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: TeleportHQ offers a free plan with no credit card required, including 3 projects, up to 10 code views or downloads, unlimited collaborators, and free hosting on a TeleportHQ subdomain, along with access to the AI website builder and Figma integration and an allotment of AI tokens for generation.

The Professional plan costs roughly 9 dollars per editor per month when billed annually, or 18 dollars per editor per month billed monthly, and adds unlimited projects, unlimited code exports, private projects, and video uploads. The Agency plan supports businesses managing many projects or domains, adding 10 or more custom domains, custom storage and bandwidth, and Vercel integration. Enterprise pricing is custom and requires contacting TeleportHQ's sales team.

features.title: Team collaboration
features.description: Unlimited collaborators supported even on the free plan.

features.title: Custom domain publishing
features.description: Connect custom domains on paid plans, with support for 10 or more domains on the Agency plan.

features.title: Vercel integration
features.description: Deploy exported projects directly through Vercel on the Agency plan.

pros: Exports genuinely production-ready, framework-native code rather than locking users into a proprietary format
Generous free plan includes unlimited team collaborators and AI website generation
Supports multiple popular frameworks (React, Vue, Next.js, Angular), giving development teams flexibility
Figma import streamlines the handoff from design tools into working code
Affordable entry-level paid plan compared to many design-to-code competitors
cons: Free plan limits projects to 3 and code exports to 10, which can be restrictive for active users
Smaller company and community compared to larger no-code platforms, which may mean fewer third-party integrations
Name similarity to the unrelated company Teleport (goteleport.com) can cause confusion in search results
Advanced hosting features like multiple custom domains require the higher-priced Agency plan
AI-generated layouts typically require manual refinement before they are production-ready

faqs.question: What is TeleportHQ used for
faqs.answer: TeleportHQ is used to visually design web interfaces and export the result as production-ready code in frameworks such as React, Vue, Next.js, Angular, or plain HTML and CSS.

faqs.question: Is TeleportHQ free
faqs.answer: Yes, TeleportHQ offers a free plan with 3 projects, up to 10 code views or downloads, unlimited collaborators, and free subdomain hosting, with no credit card required.

faqs.question: Is TeleportHQ the same company as Teleport (goteleport.com)
faqs.answer: No, they are completely unrelated companies. TeleportHQ is a Romania-based no-code visual web app builder, while Teleport (goteleport.com) is a US-based infrastructure access security company.

faqs.question: Who founded TeleportHQ
faqs.answer: TeleportHQ was founded in 2017 by CEO Paul Brie and is headquartered in Cluj-Napoca, Romania.

faqs.question: What code does TeleportHQ export
faqs.answer: TeleportHQ exports production-ready code in React, Vue, Next.js, Angular, and plain HTML and CSS.

faqs.question: Does TeleportHQ integrate with Figma

faqs.question: How much does TeleportHQ cost
faqs.answer: The Professional plan costs roughly 9 dollars per editor per month billed annually, or 18 dollars per editor per month billed monthly. An Agency plan and custom Enterprise pricing are also available.

faqs.question: Has TeleportHQ raised funding
faqs.answer: Yes, TeleportHQ raised a 2.5 million dollar seed round in 2022 led by One Capital, with co-financing from the European Fund for Regional Development.

enrichment.short_description: Tella is an all-in-one screen recording and video creation tool for making product demos, tutorials, and courses, with built-in editing, transcription, and hosting.
enrichment.seo_title: Tella Review: Screen Recording Software Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Tella is an all-in-one screen recorder for demos and tutorials. See features, pricing plans, pros, and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Tella is an all-in-one screen recording and video creation tool founded in 2020 by Grant Shaddick and Michiel Westerbeek, built through Y Combinator and primarily based in Amsterdam, Netherlands, with additional presence in San Francisco. The product lets users record their screen, webcam, microphone, and system audio on Mac, Windows, and Chrome, then edit the resulting video directly in the browser with tools such as a timeline editor, transcript-based editing, automatic layouts, and visual effects, before hosting and sharing the video with an embedded player and analytics. It is aimed at founders, marketers, customer success and sales teams, and educators who need to quickly create product demos, tutorials, and course content without a dedicated video editor. Tella has raised a total of about 2.6 million dollars in funding. Pricing is subscription-based with no permanent free tier beyond a trial: the Pro plan is 13 dollars per user per month, the Premium plan is 19 dollars per user per month (both billed annually, roughly 50 percent cheaper than monthly billing), and a custom-priced Enterprise plan adds SSO, SCIM, and dedicated support. Premium adds custom domains, branding removal, 60 FPS export, and advanced analytics on top of Pro's unlimited recording, storage, 4K export, and support for over 100 languages in transcription.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Tella
paragraphs: Tella is an all-in-one screen recording and video creation tool built for people who need to produce polished product demos, tutorials, and course content without learning a full video editing application. It combines recording, editing, hosting, and sharing in a single browser-based workflow.

The company was founded in 2020 by Grant Shaddick and Michiel Westerbeek and went through Y Combinator. Tella's team is primarily based in Amsterdam, Netherlands, with reported additional presence in San Francisco, California, and has raised roughly 2.6 million dollars in total funding.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Tella records screen, webcam, microphone, and system audio simultaneously on Mac, Windows, and in Chrome, giving creators flexible source combinations for demos and tutorials.

Built-in editing tools include a timeline editor, transcript-based editing (cutting video by editing the text transcript), automatic camera and screen layouts, and visual effects, all without leaving the browser.
Recordings can be exported at up to 4K resolution on paid plans, and Premium adds 60 FPS export for smoother motion capture.
Finished videos are hosted directly on Tella with an embedded player, view analytics, and, on higher tiers, custom domains and removal of Tella branding.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Tella does not offer a permanent free plan; new users get a trial period before choosing a paid plan. The Pro plan costs 13 dollars per user per month, with roughly 50 percent off available on annual billing, and includes unlimited video creation and storage, full recording capabilities, transcription support in over 100 languages, and 4K export.

The Premium plan costs 19 dollars per user per month (also with an annual discount) and adds custom domain setup, domain restriction controls, removal of Tella branding, 60 FPS export, and advanced analytics with traffic source tracking. An Enterprise plan offers custom pricing with volume discounts, adding SSO and SCIM authentication, a shared Slack channel with the Tella team, and personalized onboarding.

features.description: Records screen, webcam, microphone, and system audio together on Mac, Windows, and Chrome.

features.title: 4K and 60 FPS export
features.description: Supports up to 4K resolution export, with 60 FPS export available on the Premium plan.

features.title: Built-in video hosting
features.description: Hosts finished videos with an embedded player, removing the need for a separate hosting service.

features.title: Custom domains and branding removal
features.description: Premium and Enterprise plans support custom domains and removing Tella branding from shared videos.

features.title: Multi-language transcription
features.description: Automatic transcription supports more than 100 languages.

features.title: Video analytics
features.description: Tracks view counts, watch history, and, on Premium, traffic sources for shared videos.

pros: All-in-one workflow combining recording, editing, hosting, and sharing without separate tools
Transcript-based editing makes cutting and rearranging footage fast and approachable for non-editors
Supports 4K export and multi-language transcription even on the entry-level paid plan
Simple, predictable per-user pricing with a significant annual billing discount
Backed by Y Combinator with a focused, founder-friendly feature set
cons: No permanent free plan, only a limited-time trial before requiring payment
Custom domains and branding removal are locked behind the higher Premium tier
Smaller company size compared to larger video tools, which may affect long-term roadmap pace
SSO and SCIM are only available on the custom-priced Enterprise plan
Primarily focused on screen and webcam recording rather than full-featured video editing for complex projects

faqs.question: What is Tella used for
faqs.answer: Tella is used to record and edit screen, webcam, and audio content for product demos, tutorials, onboarding videos, and course content.

faqs.question: Does Tella have a free plan
faqs.answer: Tella does not offer a permanent free plan; it offers a trial period before requiring a Pro, Premium, or Enterprise subscription.

faqs.question: How much does Tella cost
faqs.answer: Tella's Pro plan is 13 dollars per user per month and the Premium plan is 19 dollars per user per month, both with a discount for annual billing; Enterprise pricing is custom.

faqs.question: Who founded Tella
faqs.answer: Tella was founded in 2020 by Grant Shaddick and Michiel Westerbeek, and went through Y Combinator.

faqs.question: Where is Tella based
faqs.answer: Tella's team is primarily based in Amsterdam, Netherlands, with additional presence reported in San Francisco, California.

faqs.question: Does Tella support 4K recording
faqs.answer: Yes, Tella supports up to 4K export on its Pro plan, with 60 FPS export added on the Premium plan.

faqs.question: Can Tella remove its own branding from videos
faqs.answer: Branding removal is available on the Premium and Enterprise plans, not on the Pro plan.

faqs.question: Does Tella support transcript-based editing
faqs.answer: Yes, Tella lets users edit videos by editing the automatically generated transcript, and supports transcription in more than 100 languages.

enrichment.short_description: Temporal is an open-source durable workflow orchestration platform that makes application code fault tolerant by automatically preserving and recovering execution state, available as a free self-hosted server or paid Temporal Cloud.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (open-source, self-hosted); Temporal Cloud from about $100/month
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Temporal is an open-source durable execution platform for fault-tolerant workflows. See features, SDKs, and Temporal Cloud pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Temporal is an open-source durable execution and workflow orchestration platform founded in 2019 by Maxim Fateev and Samar Abbas, both previously involved in building the Cadence workflow engine at Uber and earlier orchestration systems at AWS and Microsoft. The company, Temporal Technologies, is headquartered in Seattle, Washington, and has raised a total of about 350 million dollars in funding, including a 146 million dollar round in 2025 at a valuation of roughly 1.72 billion dollars, from investors including Tiger Global, Sequoia Capital, Index Ventures, and Amplify Partners. Temporal lets developers write application logic as ordinary code in languages including Go, Java, TypeScript, Python, PHP, Ruby, and .NET, while the Temporal service automatically captures execution state at every step; if a process fails, it resumes exactly where it left off without manual retry logic or complex error handling. The self-hosted, open-source server is free under the MIT license. Temporal Cloud is a managed, usage-based offering with four tiers: Essentials starting at about 100 dollars per month, Business starting at about 500 dollars per month, and custom-priced Enterprise and Mission Critical tiers, with usage billed per million actions once volume exceeds included plan allowances. Companies including Netflix, Datadog, Snap, Indeed, and Stripe use Temporal to orchestrate microservices, data pipelines, and, increasingly, AI agent workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Temporal
paragraphs: Temporal is an open-source platform for durable execution, a technique that makes application code resilient to failures by automatically preserving its execution state. Instead of writing custom retry logic, timers, and recovery code, developers write workflows as ordinary functions in a general-purpose language, and the Temporal service guarantees that execution can resume exactly where it left off after a crash, network failure, or process restart.

Temporal Technologies was founded in 2019 by Maxim Fateev and Samar Abbas, who previously built the Cadence workflow engine at Uber and worked on earlier orchestration systems at AWS (Simple Workflow Service) and Microsoft (Azure Durable Functions). The company is headquartered in Seattle, Washington, and has raised roughly 350 million dollars in funding at a valuation of approximately 1.72 billion dollars.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Workflows and Activities are the core building blocks: a Workflow orchestrates one or more Activities, which are functions that interact with external systems such as APIs, databases, or other services.

Temporal maintains a complete Event History of every step in a workflow's execution, which the service uses to replay and resume workflows precisely after any failure, without lost progress or orphaned processes.

The platform offers official SDKs for Go, Java, TypeScript, Python, PHP, Ruby, and .NET, and supports polyglot use, letting different services within the same organization use different languages against the same Temporal service.

Temporal supports long-running and human-in-the-loop workflows through Signals, Queries, and Updates, making it suitable for processes that can span minutes, days, or months, including increasingly common AI agent orchestration use cases.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Temporal's core server is free and open source under the MIT license, and organizations can self-host it at no licensing cost, though they take on the operational burden of running and scaling the service themselves.

Temporal Cloud is the managed alternative, priced across four tiers: Essentials starts at about 100 dollars per month and includes an allotment such as 1 million Actions, 1 GB of active storage, and 40 GB of retained storage; Business starts at about 500 dollars per month with higher included usage and support. Enterprise and Mission Critical tiers are custom-priced for large-scale or compliance-sensitive deployments. Usage beyond included allowances is billed at roughly 50 dollars per million Actions, and Temporal offers 1,000 dollars in free trial credits, plus up to 6,000 dollars in credits for early-stage startups.

features.title: Durable execution
features.description: Automatically preserves workflow state so execution can resume exactly where it left off after a failure.

features.title: Multi-language SDKs
features.description: Official SDKs for Go, Java, TypeScript, Python, PHP, Ruby, and .NET support polyglot teams.

features.title: Event history and replay
features.description: Records every workflow step, enabling precise recovery without custom error-handling code.

features.title: Workflows and Activities model
features.description: Orchestrates external calls through Activities, coordinated by durable Workflow functions.

features.title: Signals, Queries, and Updates
features.description: Supports long-running, human-in-the-loop workflows that react to external events over time.

features.title: Self-hosted open source server
features.description: Free, MIT-licensed server that organizations can run on their own infrastructure.

features.title: Temporal Cloud
features.description: Fully managed, usage-based hosted offering with tiered plans and support.

features.title: AI agent orchestration
features.description: Increasingly used to orchestrate multi-step, fault-tolerant AI agent workflows in production.

pros: Removes the need to hand-write complex retry, timeout, and recovery logic for distributed systems
Free, open-source self-hosted option under the MIT license with no licensing cost
Broad language support (Go, Java, TypeScript, Python, PHP, Ruby, .NET) fits polyglot engineering teams
Built by the team behind Uber's Cadence engine, with deep experience in production workflow orchestration
Adopted by well-known companies (Netflix, Datadog, Snap, Stripe) validating production scalability
cons: Self-hosting the open-source server is operationally demanding and requires dedicated engineering effort
Temporal Cloud usage-based pricing can become expensive at high action volumes without careful monitoring
Learning curve exists around Temporal's Workflow and Activity programming model for teams new to durable execution
Enterprise and Mission Critical tiers require a custom sales quote rather than transparent published pricing
Best suited to engineering-heavy organizations; less approachable for non-technical or very small teams

faqs.question: What is Temporal used for
faqs.answer: Temporal is used to build fault-tolerant, durable workflows and microservice orchestration, ensuring application code recovers automatically from failures without custom retry logic.

faqs.question: Is Temporal free
faqs.answer: Yes, Temporal's core server is free and open source under the MIT license and can be self-hosted at no licensing cost. Temporal Cloud is a paid, managed alternative.

faqs.question: Who founded Temporal
faqs.answer: Temporal was founded in 2019 by Maxim Fateev and Samar Abbas, who previously built the Cadence workflow engine at Uber.

faqs.question: What programming languages does Temporal support
faqs.answer: Temporal offers official SDKs for Go, Java, TypeScript, Python, PHP, Ruby, and .NET.

faqs.question: How much does Temporal Cloud cost
faqs.answer: Temporal Cloud starts with an Essentials plan at about 100 dollars per month and a Business plan at about 500 dollars per month, with custom pricing for Enterprise and Mission Critical tiers.

faqs.question: What is durable execution
faqs.answer: Durable execution is a technique where a system automatically preserves the state of running code so it can resume exactly where it left off after a crash or failure, without manual recovery logic.

faqs.question: Where is Temporal headquartered
faqs.answer: Temporal Technologies is headquartered in Seattle, Washington, United States.

faqs.question: Is Temporal used for AI agent workflows
faqs.answer: Yes, Temporal is increasingly used to orchestrate multi-step, fault-tolerant AI agent workflows in production, in addition to traditional microservice orchestration.

use_cases.title: Microservices orchestration
use_cases.description: Engineering teams use Temporal to coordinate calls across multiple microservices with automatic failure recovery.

use_cases.title: Long-running business processes
use_cases.description: Companies use Temporal to model long-running processes like order fulfillment or onboarding that span minutes to months.
use_cases.audience: Companies with complex, multi-step business workflows
